sentences.text: This chapter describes the Vector Cryptography extensions to the RISC-V Instruction Set Architecture.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0

sentences.text: This chapter describes the RISC-V vector cryptography extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction

sentences.text: All instructions described here are based on the Vector registers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction

sentences.text: The instructions are designed to be highly performant, with large application and server-class cores being the main target. describes cryptographic instructions for smaller cores which do not implement the vector extension.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction

sentences.text: Cryptography is a specialized subject, requiring people with many different backgrounds to cooperate in its secure and efficient implementation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Where possible, we have written this specification to be understandable by all, though we recognize that the motivations and references to algorithms or other specifications and standards may be unfamiliar to those who are not domain experts.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: This specification anticipates being read and acted on by various people with different backgrounds.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We have tried to capture these backgrounds here, with a brief explanation of what we expect them to know, and how it relates to the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We hope this aids people's understanding of which aspects of the specification are particularly relevant to them, and which they may (safely!) ignore or pass to a colleague.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Cryptographers and cryptographic software developers:: These are the people we expect to write code using the instructions in this specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: They should understand the motivations for the instructions we include, and be familiar with most of the algorithms and outside standards to which we refer.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Computer architects:: We do not expect architects to have a cryptography background.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We nonetheless expect architects to be able to examine our instructions for implementation issues, understand how the instructions will be used in context, and advise on how best to fit the functionality the cryptographers want.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Digital design engineers & micro-architects:: These are the people who will implement the specification inside a core.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Again, no cryptography expertise is assumed, but we expect them to interpret the specification and anticipate any hardware implementation issues, e.g., where high-frequency design considerations apply, or where latency/area tradeoffs exist etc.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: In particular, they should be aware of the literature around efficiently implementing AES and SM4 SBoxes in hardware.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: Verification engineers:: These people are responsible for ensuring the correct implementation of the extensions in hardware.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: No cryptography background is assumed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: We expect them to identify interesting test cases from the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: An understanding of their real-world usage will help with this.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: These are by no means the only people concerned with the specification, but they are the ones we considered most while writing it.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Intended Audience

sentences.text: RISC-V maintains a link:https://github.com/riscv/sail-riscv[formal model] of the ISA specification, implemented in the Sail ISA specification language cite:[sail].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Note that Sail refers to the specification language itself, and that there is a model of RISC-V, written using Sail.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: It was our intention to include actual Sail code in this specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: However, the Vector Crypto Sail model needs the Vector Sail model as a basis on which to build.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: This Vector Cryptography extensions specification was completed before there was an approved RISC-V Vector Sail Model.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Therefore, we don't have any Sail code to include in the instruction descriptions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Instead we have included Sail-like pseudocode.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: While we have endeavored to adhere to Sail syntax, we have taken some liberties for the sake of simplicity where we believe that that our intent is clear to the reader.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: For the sake of brevity, our pseudocode does not include the handling of masks or tail elements.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: We follow the undisturbed and agnostic policies for masks and tails as described in the *RISC-V "V" Vector Extension* specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Furthermore, the code does not explicitly handle overlap and SEW constraints; these are, however, explicitly stated in the text.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: In many cases the pseudocode includes calls to supporting functions which are too verbose to include directly in the specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: This supporting code is listed in .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: The link:https://alasdair.github.io/manual.html[Sail Manual] is recommended reading in order to best understand the code snippets.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: Also, link:https://github.com/billmcspadden-riscv/sail/blob/cookbookbr/cookbook/doc/TheSailCookbookComplete.pdf[The Sail Programming Language: A Sail Cookbook] is a good reference.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: For the latest RISC-V Sail model, refer to the formal model GitHub link:https://github.com/riscv/sail-riscv[repository].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Sail Specifications

sentences.text: In creating this extension, we tried to adhere to the following policies:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Where there is a choice between: 1) supporting diverse implementation strategies for an algorithm or 2) supporting a single implementation style which is more performant / less expensive; the vector crypto extensions will pick the more constrained but performant option.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: This fits a common pattern in other parts of the RISC-V specifications, where recommended (but not required) instruction sequences for performing particular tasks are given as an example, such that both hardware and software implementers can optimize for only a single use-case.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: The extensions will be designed to support existing standardized cryptographic constructs well.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: It will not try to support proposed standards, or cryptographic constructs which exist only in academia.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Cryptographic standards which are settled upon concurrently with or after the RISC-V vector cryptographic extensions standardization will be dealt with by future RISC-V vector cryptographic standard extensions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Historically, there has been some discussion cite:[LSYRR:04] on how newly supported operations in general-purpose computing might enable new bases for cryptographic algorithms.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: The standard will not try to anticipate new useful low-level operations which may be useful as building blocks for future cryptographic constructs.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Regarding side-channel countermeasures: Where relevant, proposed instructions must aim to remove the possibility of any timing side-channels.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: All instructions shall be implemented with data-independent timing.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: That is, the latency of the execution of these instructions shall not vary with different input values.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Policies

sentences.text: Many vector crypto instructions operate on operands that are wider than elements (which are currently limited to 64 bits wide).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: Typically, these operands are 128- and 256-bits wide.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: In many cases, these operands are comprised of smaller operands that are combined (for example, each SHA-2 operand is comprised of 4 words).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: However, in other cases these operands are a single value (for example, in the AES round instructions, each operand is 128-bit block or round key).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: We treat these operands as a vector of one or more element groups
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: Each vector crypto instruction that operates on element groups explicitly specifies their three defining parameters: EGW, EGS, and EEW.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: | | Instruction Group | Extension | EGW | EEW | EGS
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups
Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: For all of the vector crypto instructions in this specification, `EEW`=`SEW`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: A *Vector Element Group* is a vector of one or more element groups. - A *Scalar Element Group* is a single element group.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: Element groups can be formed across registers in implementations where `VLEN`< `EGW` by using an `LMUL`>1.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: As with all vector instructions, the number of elements processed is specified by the vector length `vl`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: The number of element groups operated upon is then `vl`/`EGS`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: Likewise the starting element group is `vstart`/`EGS`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: See for limitations on `vl` and `vstart` for vector crypto instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Element Groups

sentences.text: All standard vector instruction constraints specified by RVV 1.0 apply to Vector Crypto instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: In addition to those constraints a few additional specific constraints are introduced.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: The following is a quick reference for the various constraints of specific Vector Crypto instructions.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: vl and vstart constraints:: Since `vl` and `vstart` refer to elements, Vector Crypto instructions that use elements groups (See ) require that these values are an integer multiple of the Element Group Size (`EGS`).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: Instructions that violate the `vl` or `vstart` requirements are reserved.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: | vaes* | 4 | vsha2* | 4 | vg* | 4 | vsm3* | 8 | vsm4* | 4
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: LMUL constraints:: For element-group instructions, `LMUL`*`VLEN` must always be at least as large as `EGW`, otherwise an illegal-instruction exception is raised, even if `vl`=0.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: In such a case all other `SEW` values are reserved.

sentences.text: Vector/Scalar constraints:: This specification defines new vector/scalar (.vs) instructions that uses *Scalar Element Groups*.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: The *Scalar Element Group* operand has `EMUL = ceil(EGW / VLEN)`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: In the case of the `.vs` instructions defined in this specification, `vs2` holds a 128-bit scalar element group.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: For implementations with `VLEN` ≥ 128, `vs2` refers to a single register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: Thus, the `vd` register group must not overlap the `vs2` register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: However, in implementations where `VLEN` < 128, `vs2` refers to a register group comprised of the number of registers needed to hold the 128-bit scalar element group.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: In this case, the `vd` register group must not overlap this `vs2` register group.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: | | Instruction | Register | Cannot Overlap
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Instruction Constraints

sentences.text: The RISC-V Vector Extension defines three encodings for Vector-Scalar operations which get their scalar operand from a GPR or FP register:
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: OPIVX: Scalar GPR x register - OPFVF: Scalar FP f register - OPMVX: Scalar GPR x register
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: However, the Vector Extensions include Vector Reduction Operations which can also be considered Vector-Scalar operations because a scalar operand is provided from element 0 of vector register `vs1`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: The vector operand is provided in vector register group `vs2`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: These reduction operations all use the `.vs` suffix in their mnemonics.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: Additionally, the reduction operations all produce a scalar result in element 0 of the destination register, `vd`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: The Vector Crypto Extensions define Vector-Scalar instructions that are similar to these Vector Reduction Operations in that they get a scalar operand from a vector register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: However, they differ in that they get a scalar element group (see ) from `vs2` and they return vector results to `vd`, which is also a source vector operand.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: These Vector-Scalar crypto instructions also use the `.vs` suffix in their mnemonics.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: These instructions enable a single key, specified as a scalar element group in `vs2`, to be applied to each element group of register group `vd`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Vector-Scalar Instructions

sentences.text: The following contains some guidelines that enable the portability of vector-crypto-based code to implementations with different values for `VLEN`
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Application Processors:: Application processors are expected to follow the V-extension and will therefore have `VLEN` {ge} 128.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: However, the SHA-512 and SM3 instructions have an `EGW`=256.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Implementations with `VLEN` = 128, require that `LMUL` is doubled for these instructions in order to create 256-bit elements across a pair of registers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Code written with this doubling of `LMUL` will not affect the results returned by implementations with `VLEN` {ge} 256 because `vl` controls how many element groups are processed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Therefore, we recommend that libraries that implement SHA-512 and SM3 employ this doubling of `LMUL` to ensure that the software can run on all implementation with `VLEN` {ge} 128.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: While the doubling of `LMUL` for these instructions is safe for implementations with `VLEN` {ge} 256, it may be less optimal as it will result in unnecessary register pressure and might exact a performance penalty in some microarchitectures.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Therefore, we suggest that in addition to providing portable code for SHA-512 and SM3, libraries should also include more optimal code for these instructions when `VLEN` {ge} 256. //
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: | | Algorithm | Instructions | VLEN | LMUL
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability
Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: Embedded processors will typically have implementations with `VLEN` < 128.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: This will require code to be written with larger `LMUL` values to enable the element groups to be formed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: The `.vs` instructions require scalar element groups of `EGW`=128.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: On implementations with `VLEN` < 128, these scalar element groups will necessarily be formed across registers.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: This is different from most scalars in vector instructions that typically consume part of a single register.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Introduction > Software Portability

sentences.text: The section introduces all of the extensions in the Vector Cryptography Instruction Set Extension Specification.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: The and Vector Crypto Extensions --and accordingly the composite extensions , , , and -- depend on Zve64x.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: All of the other Vector Crypto Extensions depend on `Zve32x`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: `Zvknhb` implies `Zvknha`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: If `Zve32x` is supported then `Zvkb` or `Zvbb` provide support for EEW of 8, 16, and 32.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: If `Zve64x` is supported then `Zvkb` or `Zvbb` also add support for EEW 64.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: All cryptography-specific instructions defined in this Vector Crypto specification (i.e., those in , , , and but not ,, or ) shall be executed with data-independent execution latency as defined in the .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: It is important to note that the Vector Crypto instructions are independent of the implementation of the `Zkt` extension and do not require that `Zkt` is implemented.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: This specification includes a extension that, when implemented, requires certain vector instructions (including , , and ) to be executed with data-independent execution latency.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: Detection of individual cryptography extensions uses the unified software-based RISC-V discovery method.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview

sentences.text: The Zvkt extension requires all implemented instructions from the following list to be executed with data-independent execution latency as defined in the .
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview > `Zvkt` - Vector Data-Independent Execution Latency

sentences.text: Data-independent execution latency (DIEL) applies to all data operands of an instruction, even those that are not a part of the body or that are inactive.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview > `Zvkt` - Vector Data-Independent Execution Latency

sentences.text: However, DIEL does not apply to other values such as vl, vtype, and the mask (when used to control execution of a masked vector instruction).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview > `Zvkt` - Vector Data-Independent Execution Latency

sentences.text: Also, DIEL does not apply to constant values specified in the instruction encoding such as the use of the zero register (`x0`), and, in the case of immediate forms of an instruction, the values in the immediate fields (i.e., imm, and uimm).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview > `Zvkt` - Vector Data-Independent Execution Latency

sentences.text: In some cases --- which are explicitly specified in the lists below --- operands that are used as control rather than data are exempt from DIEL.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Extensions Overview > `Zvkt` - Vector Data-Independent Execution Latency

sentences.text: Description:: A final-round AES block cipher decryption is performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: The InvShiftRows and InvSubBytes steps are applied to each round state element group from `vd`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: This is then XORed with the round key in either the corresponding element group in `vs2` (vector-vector form) or scalar element group in `vs2` (vector-scalar form).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated upon.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (VAESDF(vs2, vd, suffix)) = { if(LMUL*VLEN < EGW) then { handleillegal(); // illegal-instruction exception RETIREFAIL } else {
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: eglen = (vl/EGS) egstart = (vstart/EGS)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: foreach (i from egstart to eglen-1) { let keyelem = if suffix "vv" then i else 0; let state : bits(128) = getvelem(vd, EGW=128, i); let rkey : bits(128) = getvelem(vs2, EGW=128, keyelem); let sr : bits(128) = aesshiftrowsinv(state); let sb : bits(128) = aessubbytesinv(sr); let ark : bits(128) = sb ^ rkey; setvelem(vd, EGW=128, i, ark); } RETIRESUCCESS } } --
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesdf.[vv,vs]

sentences.text: Description:: A middle-round AES block cipher decryption is performed.

sentences.text: Description:: A single round of the forward AES-128 KeySchedule is performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: The round number, which ranges from 1 to 10, comes from `uimm[3:0]`; `uimm[4]` is ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: The out-of-range `uimm[3:0]` values of 0 and 11-15 are mapped to in-range values by inverting `uimm[3]`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: Thus, 0 maps to 8, and 11-15 maps to 3-7.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: The round number is used to specify a round constant which is used in generating the first round key word.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated upon.

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaeskf1.vi

sentences.text: foreach (i from egstart to eglen-1) { let CurrentRoundKey[3:0] : bits(128) = getvelem(vs2, EGW=128, i); let w[0] : bits(32) = aessubwordfwd(aesrotword(CurrentRoundKey[3])) XOR aesdecodercon(r) XOR CurrentRoundKey[0] let w[1] : bits(32) = w[0] XOR CurrentRoundKey[1] let w[2] : bits(32) = w[1] XOR CurrentRoundKey[2] let w[3] : bits(32) = w[2] XOR CurrentRoundKey[3] setvelem(vd, EGW=128, i, w[3:0]); } RETIRESUCCESS } }

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vaesz.vs

sentences.text: There is only a `.vs` form of the instruction. `Vs2` holds a scalar element group that is used as the round key for all of the round state element groups.

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vandn.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: Specifically, the Chi step of the FIPS 202 Keccak Permutation.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vandn.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: Emulating it via 2 instructions is expected to have significant performance impact.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vandn.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: The `.vv` form of the instruction is what is needed for SHA3; the `.vx` form was added for completeness.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vandn.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (VANDN(vs2, vs1, vd, suffix)) = { foreach (i from vstart to vl-1) { let op1 = match suffix { "vv" => getvelem(vs1, SEW, i), "vx" => sextortruncatetosew(X(vs1)) }; let op2 = getvelem(vs2, SEW, i); setvelem(vd, EEW=SEW, i, ~op1 & op2); } RETIRESUCCESS }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vandn.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: Description:: Produces the low half of 128-bit carry-less product.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vclmul.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: Each 64-bit element in the `vs2` vector register is carry-less multiplied by either each 64-bit element in `vs1` (vector-vector), or the 64-bit value from integer register `rs1` (vector-scalar).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vclmul.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: The result is the least significant 64 bits of the carry-less product.

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vclmul.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: foreach (i from vstart to vl-1) { let op1 : bits (64) = if suffix "vv" then getvelem(vs1,i) else zextortruncatetosew(X(vs1)); let op2 : bits (64) = getvelem(vs2,i); let product : bits (64) = clmul(op1,op2,SEW); setvelem(vd, i, product); } RETIRESUCCESS }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vclmul.[vv,vx]

sentences.text: function clmul(x, y, width) = { let result : bits(width) = zeros(); foreach (i from 0 to (width - 1)) { if y[i] 1 then result = result ^ (x << i); } result } --

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: Encoding:: [wavedrom, , svg] .... {reg:[ {bits: 7, name: 'OP-VE'}, {bits: 5, name: 'vd'}, {bits: 3, name: 'OPMVV'}, {bits: 5, name: 'vs1'}, {bits: 5, name: 'vs2'}, {bits: 1, name: '1'}, {bits: 6, name: '101100'}, ]} ....
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: | |Register |Direction |EGW |EGS |SEW |Definition
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: | Vd | input | 128 | 4 | 32 | Partial hash (Y~i~) | Vs1 | input | 128 | 4 | 32 | Cipher text (X~i~) | Vs2 | input | 128 | 4 | 32 | Hash Subkey (H) | Vd | output | 128 | 4 | 32 | Partial-hash (Y~i+1~) |
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: Description:: A single "iteration" of the GHASH~H~ algorithm is performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: This instruction treats all of the inputs and outputs as 128-bit polynomials and performs operations over GF[2].
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: It produces the next partial hash (Y~i+1~) by adding the current partial hash (Y~i~) to the cipher text block (X~i~) and then multiplying (over GF(2^128^)) this sum by the Hash Subkey (H).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: The multiplication over GF(2^128^) is a carry-less multiply of two 128-bit polynomials modulo GHASH's irreducible polynomial (x^128^ + x^7^ + x^2^ + x + 1).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: The operation can be compactly defined as // Y~i+1~ = (Y~i~ {interpunct} H) ^ X~i~ Y~i+1~ = ((Y~i~ ^ X~i~) {interpunct} H)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: The NIST specification (see ) orders the coefficients from left to right x~0~x~1~x~2~...x~127~ for a polynomial x~0~ + x~1~u +x~2~ u^2^ + ... + x~127~u^127^.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: This can be viewed as a collection of byte elements in memory with the byte containing the lowest coefficients (i.e., 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7) residing at the lowest memory address.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: Since the bits in the bytes are reversed, this instruction internally performs bit swaps within bytes to put the bits in the standard ordering (e.g., 7,6,5,4,3,2,1,0).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated upon.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,pseudocode] -- function clause execute (VGHSH(vs2, vs1, vd)) = { // operands are input with bits reversed in each byte if(LMUL*VLEN < EGW) then { handleillegal(); // illegal-instruction exception RETIREFAIL } else {
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: eglen = (vl/EGS) egstart = (vstart/EGS)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: foreach (i from egstart to eglen-1) { let Y = getvelem(vd,EGW=128,i); // current partial-hash let X = getvelem(vs1,EGW=128,i); // block cipher output let H = brev8(getvelem(vs2,EGW=128,i)); // Hash subkey
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: for (int bit = 0; bit < 128; bit++) { if bittobool(S[bit]) Z ^= H
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: bool reduce = bittobool(H[127]); H = H << 1; // left shift H by 1 if (reduce) H ^= 0x87; // Reduce using x^7 + x^2 + x^1 + 1 polynomial }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: let result = brev8(Z); // bit reverse bytes to get back to GCM standard ordering setvelem(vd, EGW=128, i, result); } RETIRESUCCESS } } --
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vghsh.vv

sentences.text: Description:: A GHASH~H~ multiply is performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: This instruction treats all of the inputs and outputs as 128-bit polynomials and performs operations over GF[2].

sentences.text: The multiplication over GF(2^128^) is a carry-less multiply of two 128-bit polynomials modulo GHASH's irreducible polynomial (x^128^ + x^7^ + x^2^ + x + 1).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: The NIST specification (see ) orders the coefficients from left to right x~0~x~1~x~2~...x~127~ for a polynomial x~0~ + x~1~u +x~2~ u^2^ + ... + x~127~u^127^.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: This can be viewed as a collection of byte elements in memory with the byte containing the lowest coefficients (i.e., 0,1,2,3,4,5,6,7) residing at the lowest memory address.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: Since the bits in the bytes are reversed, This instruction internally performs bit swaps within bytes to put the bits in the standard ordering (e.g., 7,6,5,4,3,2,1,0).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: This instruction must always be implemented such that its execution latency does not depend on the data being operated upon.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vgmul.vv

sentences.text: Description:: - `SEW`=32: 2 rounds of SHA-256 compression are performed (`zvknha` and `zvknhb`) - `SEW`=64: 2 rounds of SHA-512 compression are performed (`zvknhb`)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2c[hl].vv

sentences.text: Two words of `vs1` are processed with the 8 words of current state held in `vd` and `vs2` to perform two rounds of hash computation producing four words of the next state.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2c[hl].vv

sentences.text: [NOTE] .Note to software developers The NIST standard (see ) requires the final hash to be in big-endian byte ordering within SEW-sized words.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2c[hl].vv

sentences.text: Since this instruction treats all words as little-endian, software needs to perform an endian swap on the final output of this instruction after all of the message blocks have been processed.

sentences.text: Description:: - `SEW`=32: Four rounds of SHA-256 message schedule expansion are performed (`zvknha` and `zvknhb`) - `SEW`=64: Four rounds of SHA-512 message schedule expansion are performed (`zvknhb`)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: Eleven of the last 16 `SEW`-sized message-schedule words from `vd` (oldest), `vs2`, and `vs1` (most recent) are processed to produce the next 4 message-schedule words.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: [NOTE] .Note to software developers The first 16 SEW-sized words of the message schedule come from the message block in big-endian byte order.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: Since this instruction treats all words as little endian, software is required to endian swap these words.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: All of the subsequent message schedule words are produced by this instruction and therefore do not require an endian swap.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: [NOTE] .Note to software developers Software is required to pack the words into element groups as shown above in the arguments table.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsha2ms.vv

sentences.text: The indices indicate the relate age with lower indices indicating older words. // [NOTE] // // W~13~ is not used by the instruction. //

sentences.text: Description:: Two rounds of SM3 compression are performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: The current state of eight 32-bit words is read in as an element group from `vd`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: Eight 32-bit message words are read in as an element group from `vs2`, although only four of them are used.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: All of the 32-bit input words are byte-swapped from big endian to little endian.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: These inputs are processed somewhat differently based on the round group (as specified in rnds), and the next state is generated as an element group of eight 32-bit words.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: The next state of eight 32-bit words are generated, swapped from little endian to big endian, and are returned in an eight-element group.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: The round number is provided by the 5-bit `rnds` unsigned immediate.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: Legal values are 0 - 31 and indicate which group of two rounds are being performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: For example, if rnds=1, then rounds 2 and 3 are being performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm3c.vi

sentences.text: Description:: Four rounds of the SM4 Key Expansion are performed.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: Four round keys are read in as a 4-element group from `vs2`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: Each of the next four round keys are generated by iteratively XORing the last three round keys with a constant that is indexed by the Round Group Number, performing a byte-wise substitution, and then performing XORs between rotated versions of this value and the corresponding current round key.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: The Round group number (`rnd`) comes from `uimm[2:0]`; the bits in `uimm[4:3]` are ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: Round group numbers range from 0 to 7 and indicate which group of four round keys are being generated.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: Round Keys range from 0-31.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vsm4k.vi

sentences.text: For example, if `rnd`=1, then round keys 4, 5, 6, and 7 are being generated.

sentences.text: Description:: A widening logical shift left is performed on each element of `vs2`.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vwsll.[vv,vx,vi]

sentences.text: The elements in `vs2` are zero-extended to 2*`SEW` bits, then shifted left by the shift amount specified by either the corresponding elements of `vs1` (vector-vector), integer register `rs1` (vector-scalar), or an immediate value (vector-immediate).
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vwsll.[vv,vx,vi]

sentences.text: Only the low log2(2*`SEW`) bits of the shift-amount value are used, all other bits are ignored.
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vwsll.[vv,vx,vi]

sentences.text: Operation:: [source,sail] -- function clause execute (VWSLLVV(vs2, vs1, vd)) = { foreach (i from vstart to vl - 1) { setvelem(vd, EEW=2*SEW, i, getvelem(vs2, i) << (getvelem(vs1, i) & ((2*SEW)-1)) ) } RETIRESUCCESS }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vwsll.[vv,vx,vi]

sentences.text: function clause execute (VWSLLVX(vs2, rs1, vd)) = { foreach (i from vstart to vl - 1) { setvelem(vd, EEW=2*SEW, i, getvelem(vs2, i) << (X(rs1) & ((2*SEW)-1)) ) } RETIRESUCCESS }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Instructions > vwsll.[vv,vx,vi]

sentences.text: function clause execute (VWSLLVI(vs2, uimm[4:0], vd)) = { foreach (i from vstart to vl - 1) { setvelem(vd, EEW=2*SEW, i, getvelem(vs2, i) << (uimm[4:0] & ((2*SEW)-1)) ) } RETIRESUCCESS } --

sentences.text: [source,sail] ---- val xt2 : bits(8) -> bits(8) function xt2(x) = { (x << 1) ^ (if bittobool(x[7]) then 0x1b else 0x00) }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val xt3 : bits(8) -> bits(8) function xt3(x) = x ^ xt2(x)
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val gfmul : (bits(8), bits(4)) -> bits(8) function gfmul( x, y) = { (if bittobool(y[0]) then x else 0x00) ^ (if bittobool(y[1]) then xt2( x) else 0x00) ^ (if bittobool(y[2]) then xt2(xt2( x)) else 0x00) ^ (if bittobool(y[3]) then xt2(xt2(xt2(x))) else 0x00) }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val aesmixcolumnbytefwd : bits(8) -> bits(32) function aesmixcolumnbytefwd(so) = { gfmul(so, 0x3) @ so @ so @ gfmul(so, 0x2) }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val aesmixcolumnbyteinv : bits(8) -> bits(32) function aesmixcolumnbyteinv(so) = { gfmul(so, 0xb) @ gfmul(so, 0xd) @ gfmul(so, 0x9) @ gfmul(so, 0xe) }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val aesmixcolumnfwd : bits(32) -> bits(32) function aesmixcolumnfwd(x) = { let s0 : bits (8) = x[ 7.. 0]; let s1 : bits (8) = x[15.. 8]; let s2 : bits (8) = x[23..16]; let s3 : bits (8) = x[31..24]; let b0 : bits (8) = xt2(s0) ^ xt3(s1) ^ (s2) ^ (s3); let b1 : bits (8) = (s0) ^ xt2(s1) ^ xt3(s2) ^ (s3); let b2 : bits (8) = (s0) ^ (s1) ^ xt2(s2) ^ xt3(s3); let b3 : bits (8) = xt3(s0) ^ (s1) ^ (s2) ^ xt2(s3); b3 @ b2 @ b1 @ b0 }
sentences.section: Preamble > Cryptography Extensions: Vector Instructions, Version 1.0 > Supporting Sail Code

sentences.text: val aesmixcolumninv : bits(32) -> bits(32) function aesmixcolumninv(x) = { let s0 : bits (8) = x[ 7.. 0]; let s1 : bits (8) = x[15.. 8]; let s2 : bits (8) = x[23..16]; let s3 : bits (8) = x[31..24]; let b0 : bits (8) = gfmul(s0, 0xE) ^ gfmul(s1, 0xB) ^ gfmul(s2, 0xD) ^ gfmul(s3, 0x9); let b1 : bits (8) = gfmul(s0, 0x9) ^ gfmul(s1, 0xE) ^ gfmul(s2, 0xB) ^ gfmul(s3, 0xD); let b2 : bits (8) = gfmul(s0, 0xD) ^ gfmul(s1, 0x9) ^ gfmul(s2, 0xE) ^ gfmul(s3, 0xB); let b3 : bits (8) = gfmul(s0, 0xB) ^ gfmul(s1, 0xD) ^ gfmul(s2, 0x9) ^ gfmul(s3, 0xE); b3 @ b2 @ b1 @ b0 }

sentences.text: val aesshiftrowsfwd : bits(128) -> bits(128) function aesshiftrowsfwd(x) = { let ic3 : bits(32) = aesgetcolumn(x, 3); let ic2 : bits(32) = aesgetcolumn(x, 2); let ic1 : bits(32) = aesgetcolumn(x, 1); let ic0 : bits(32) = aesgetcolumn(x, 0); let oc0 : bits(32) = ic3[31..24] @ ic2[23..16] @ ic1[15.. 8] @ ic0[ 7.. 0]; let oc1 : bits(32) = ic0[31..24] @ ic3[23..16] @ ic2[15.. 8] @ ic1[ 7.. 0]; let oc2 : bits(32) = ic1[31..24] @ ic0[23..16] @ ic3[15.. 8] @ ic2[ 7.. 0]; let oc3 : bits(32) = ic2[31..24] @ ic1[23..16] @ ic0[15.. 8] @ ic3[ 7.. 0]; (oc3 @ oc2 @ oc1 @ oc0) }

sentences.text: val brev : bits(SEW) -> bits(SEW) function brev(x) = { let output : bits(SEW) = 0; foreach (i from 0 to SEW-8 by 8) output[i+7..i] = reversebitsinbyte(input[i+7..i]); output }

sentences.text: val rev8 : bits(SEW) -> bits(SEW) function rev8(x) = { // endian swap let output : bits(SEW) = 0; let j = SEW - 1; foreach (k from 0 to (SEW - 8) by 8) { output[k..(k + 7)] = x[(j - 7)..j]; j = j - 8; output } RETIRESUCCESS
